title: Hárbarðsljóð
source: Codex Regius (GKS 2365 4to) via handrit.is + Heimskringla.no Old Norse + Thorpe (1866) English
description: Hárbarðsljóð, from the Poetic Edda preserved in Codex Regius (GKS 2365 4to, c. 1270 AD), housed at the Árni Magnússon Institute, Reykjavík. Manuscript folio scans (folios 13v–15v) from handrit.is. Old Norse text verbatim from heimskringla.no. English translation verbatim from Benjamin Thorpe's 1866 edition (Project Gutenberg #14726, public domain).
introduction:
This entry meets the strict three-criteria standard of the Theosis Library:

  1. Manuscript scan: Folios 13v–15v of Codex Regius (GKS 2365 4to), the unique 13th-century Icelandic manuscript containing the Poetic Edda, hosted by the Árni Magnússon Institute via handrit.is.
  2. Original text: The Old Norse text of Hárbarðsljóð, verbatim from heimskringla.no.
  3. English translation: Benjamin Thorpe's 1866 translation of the Elder Edda, retrieved verbatim from Project Gutenberg eBook #14726 (public domain).

translation.original_text: <sup>1</sup>"Hverr er sá sveinn sveina, / er stendr fyr sundit handan?" <sup>2</sup>"Hverr er sá karl karla, / er kallar of váginn?" <sup>3</sup>"Fer þú mik um sundit, / fæði ek þik á morgun; / meis hef ek á baki, / verðr-a matr in betri; / át ek í hvíld, / áðr ek heiman fór, / síldr ok hafra; / saðr em ek enn þess." <sup>4</sup>"Árligum verkum / hrósar þú, verðinum; / veizt-at-tu fyrir görla, / döpr eru þín heimkynni, / dauð, hygg ek, at þín móðir sé." <sup>5</sup>"Þat segir þú nú, / er hverjum þykkir / mest at vita, / at mín móðir dauð sé."
translation.text: <sup>1</sup>Rise up now, Skirnir! go and request our son to speak; and inquire with whom he so sage may be offended. _Skirnir_. <sup>2</sup>Harsh words I have from your son to fear, if I go to speak with him, and to inquire with whom he so sage may be offended. _Skirnir_. <sup>3</sup>Tell me now, Frey, prince of gods! for I desire to know, why alone thou sittest in the spacious hall the livelong day? _Frey_. <sup>4</sup>Why shall I tell thee, thou young man, my mind's great trouble? for the Alfs' illuminator shines every day, yet not for my pleasure. _Skirnir_. <sup>5</sup>Thy care cannot, I think, be so great, that to me thou canst not tell it; for in early days we were young together: well might we trust each other. _Frey_.

translation.original_text: <sup>6</sup>"Þeygi er sem þú / þrjú bú góð eigir; / berbeinn þú stendr / ok hefr brautinga gervi, / þatki, at þú hafir brækr þínar." <sup>7</sup>"Stýrðu hingat eikjunni, / ek mun þér stöðna kenna, - / eða hverr á skipit, / er þú heldr við landit?" <sup>8</sup>"Hildolfr sá heitir, / er mik halda bað, / rekkr inn ráðsvinni, / er býr í Ráðseyjarsundi; / bað-at hann hlennimenn flytja / eða hrossaþjófa, / góða eina / ok þá, er ek görva kunna; / segðu til nafns þíns, / ef þú vill um sundit fara!" <sup>9</sup>"Segja mun ek til nafns míns, / þótt ek sekr séak, / ok til alls eðlis: / Ek em Óðins sonr, / Meila bróðir, / en Magna faðir, / þrúðvaldr goða, / við Þór knáttu hér dæma! / Hins vil ek nú spyrja, / hvat þú heitir." <sup>10</sup>"Hárbarðr ek heiti, / hylk um nafn sjaldan."
translation.text: <sup>6</sup>In Gymir's courts I saw walking a maid for whom I long. Her arms gave forth light wherewith shone all air and water. <sup>7</sup>Is more desirable to me that maid than to any youth in early days; yet will no one, Æsir or Alfar, that we together live. _Skirnir_. <sup>8</sup>Give me but thy steed, which can bear me through the dusk, flickering flame, and that sword, which brandishes itself against the Jotuns' race. _Frey_. <sup>9</sup>I will give thee my steed, which can bear thee through the dusk, flickering flame, and that sword, which will itself brandish, if he is bold who raises it. _Skirnir Speaks to the Horse_. <sup>10</sup>Dark it is without, 'tis time, I say, for us to go across the misty fells, over the Thursar's land: we shall both return, or the all-potent Jotun will seize us both. Skirnir rides to Jotunheim, to Gymir's mansion, where fierce dogs were chained at the gate of the enclosure that was round Gymir's hall. He rides on to where a cowherd was sitting on a mound, and says to him:

translation.original_text: <sup>11</sup>"Hvat skaltu of nafn hylja, / nema þú sakar eigir?" <sup>12</sup>"En þótt ek sakar eiga, / þá mun ek forða fjörvi mínu / fyr slíkum sem þú ert, / nema ek feigr sé." <sup>13</sup>"Harm ljótan / mér þykkir í því / at vaða um váginn til þín / ok væta ögur minn; / skylda ek launa / kögursveini þínum / kanginyrði, / ef ek kæmumk yfir sundit." <sup>14</sup>"Hér mun ek standa / ok þín heðan bíða; / fannt-a þú mann in harðara / at Hrungni dauðan." <sup>15</sup>"Hins viltu nú geta, / er vit Hrungnir deildum, / sá inn stórúðgi jötunn, / er ór steini var höfuðit á; / þó lét ek hann falla / ok fyrir hníga. / Hvat vanntu þá meðan, Hárbarðr?"
translation.text: <sup>11</sup>Tell me, cowherd! as on the mound thou sittest, and watchest all the ways, how I to the speech may come, of the young maiden, for Gymir's dogs? _Cowherd_. <sup>12</sup>Either thou art death-doomed, or thou art a departed one. Speech wilt thou ever lack with the good maid of Gymir. _Skirnir_. <sup>13</sup>Better choices than to whine there are for him who is prepared to die: for one day was my age decreed, and my whole life determined. _Gerd_. <sup>14</sup>What is that sound of sounds, which I now sounding hear within our dwelling? The earth is shaken, and with it all the house of Gymir trembles. _A serving-maid_. <sup>15</sup>A man is here without, dismounted from his horse's back: he lets his steed browse on the grass. _Gerd_.

translation.original_text: <sup>16</sup>"Var ek með Fjölvari / fimm vetr alla / í ey þeiri, / er Algræn heitir; / vega vér þar knáttum / ok val fella, / margs at freista, / mans at kosta." <sup>17</sup>"Hversu snúnuðu yðr konur yðrar?" <sup>18</sup>"Sparkar áttu vér konur, / ef oss at spökum yrði; / horskar áttu vér konur, / ef oss hollar væri; / þær ór sandi / síma undu, / ok ór dali djúpum / grund of grófu; / varð ek þeim einn öllum / efri at ráðum; / hvílda ek hjá þeim systrum sjau, / ok hafða ek geð þeira allt ok gaman. / Hvat vanntu þá meðan, Þórr?" <sup>19</sup>"Ek drap Þjaza, / inn þrúðmóðga jötun, / upp ek varp augum / Alvalda sonar / á þann inn heiða himin; / þau eru merki mest / minna verka, / þau er allir menn síðan of séa. / Hvat vanntu þá meðan, Hárbarðr?" <sup>20</sup>"Miklar manvélar / ek hafða við myrkriður, / þá er ek vélta þær frá verum; / harðan jötun / ek hugða Hlébarð vera, / gaf hann mér gambantein, / en ek vélta hann ór viti."
translation.text: <sup>16</sup>Bid him enter into our hall, and drink of the bright mead; although I fear it is my brother's slayer who waits without. <sup>17</sup>Who is this of the Alfar's, or of the Æsir's sons, or of the wise Vanir's? Why art thou come alone, through the hostile fire, our halls to visit? _Skirnir_. <sup>18</sup>I am not of the Alfar's, nor of the Æsir's sons, nor of the wise Vanir's; yet I am come alone, through the hostile fire, your halls to visit. <sup>19</sup>Apples all-golden I have here eleven: these I will give thee, Gerd, thy love to gain, that thou mayest say that Frev to thee lives dearest. _Gerd_. <sup>20</sup>The apples eleven I never will accept for any mortal's pleasure; nor will I and Frey, while our lives last, live both together. _Skirnir_.

translation.original_text: <sup>21</sup>"Illum huga / launaðir þú þá góðar gjafar." <sup>22</sup>"Þat hefr eik, / er af annarri skefr, / of sik er hverr í slíku. / Hvat vanntu þá meðan Þórr?" <sup>23</sup>"Ek var austr / ok jötna barðak / brúðir bölvísar, / er til bjargs gengu; / mikil myndi ætt jötna, / ef allir lifði / vætr myndi manna / undir Miðgarði. / Hvat vanntu þá meðan, Hárbarðr?" <sup>24</sup>"Var ek á Vallandi / ok vígum fylgdak, / atta ek jöfrum, / en aldri sættak; / Óðinn á jarla, / þá er í val falla, / en Þórr á þrælakyn." <sup>25</sup>"Ójafnt skipta / er þú myndir með ásum liði, / ef þú ættir vilgi mikils vald."
translation.text: <sup>21</sup>The ring too I will give thee, which was burnt with the young son of Odin. Eight of equal weight will from it drop, every ninth night. _Gerd_. <sup>22</sup>The ring I will not accept, burnt though it may have been with the young son of Odin. I have no lack of gold in Gymir's courts; for my father's wealth I share. _Skirnir_. <sup>23</sup>Seest thou this sword, young maiden! thin, glittering-bright, which I have here in hand? I thy head will sever from thy neck, if thou speakst not favourably to me. _Gerd_. <sup>24</sup>Suffer compulsion will I never, to please any man; yet this I foresee, if thou and Gymir meet, ye will eagerly engage in fight. _Skirnir_. <sup>25</sup>Seest thou this sword, young maiden! thin, glittering-bright, which I have here in hand? Beneath its edge shall the old Jotun fall: thy sire is death-doomed.

translation.original_text: <sup>26</sup>"Þórr á afl ærit, / en ekki hjarta; / af hræðslu ok hugbleyði / þér var í hanzka troðit, / ok þóttisk-a þú þá Þórr vera; / hvárki þú þá þorðir / fyr hræðslu þinni / hnjósa né físa, / svá at Fjalarr heyrði." <sup>27</sup>"Hárbarðr inn ragi, / ek mynda þik í hel drepa, / ef ek mætta seilask um sund." <sup>28</sup>"Hvat skyldir um sund seilask, / er sakir ro alls engar? / Hvat vanntu þá, Þórr?" <sup>29</sup>"Ek var austr / ok ána varðak, / þá er mik sóttu / þeir Svárangs synir; / grjóti þeir mik börðu, / gagni urðu þeir þó lítt fegnir, / þó urðu þeir mik fyrri / friðar at biðja. / Hvat vanntu þá meðan, Hárbarðr?" <sup>30</sup>"Ek var austr / ok við einhverja dæmðak, / lék ek við ina línhvítu / ok launþing háðak; / gladdak ina gullbjörtu, / gamni mær unði."
translation.text: <sup>26</sup>With a taming-wand I smite thee, and I will tame thee, maiden! to my will. Thou shalt go thither, where the sons of men shall never more behold thee. <sup>27</sup>On an eagle's mount thou shalt early sit, looking and turned towards Hel. Food shall to thee more loathsome be than is to any one the glistening serpent among men. <sup>28</sup>As a prodigy thou shalt be, when thou goest forth; Hrimnir shall at thee gaze, all beings at thee stare; more wide-known thou shalt become than the watch among the gods,[37] if thou from thy gratings gape. <sup>29</sup>Solitude and disgust, bonds and impatience, shall thy tears with grief augment. Set thee down, and I will tell thee of a whelming flood of care, and a double grief. <sup>30</sup>Terrors shall bow thee down the livelong day, in the Jotuns' courts. To the Hrimthursar's halls, thou shalt each day crawl exhausted, joyless crawl; wail for pastime shalt thou have, and tears and misery.

translation.original_text: <sup>31</sup>"Góð átt þú þér mankynni þar þá." <sup>32</sup>"Liðs þíns / væra ek þá þurfi, Þórr, / at ek helda þeiri inni línhvítu mey." <sup>33</sup>"Ek munda þér þá þat veita, / ef ek viðr of kæmumk." <sup>34</sup>"Ek mynda þér þá trúa, / nema þú mik í tryggð véltir." <sup>35</sup>"Emk-at ek sá hælbítr / sem húðskór forn á vár."
translation.text: <sup>31</sup>With a three-headed Thurs thou shalt be ever bound, or be without a mate. Thy mind shall tear thee from morn to morn: as the thistle thou shalt be which has thrust itself on the house-top. <sup>32</sup>To the wold I have been, and to the humid grove, a magic wand to get. A magic wand I got. <sup>33</sup>Wroth with thee is Odin, wroth with thee is the Æsir's prince; Frey shall loathe thee, even ere thou, wicked maid! shalt have felt the gods' dire vengeance. <sup>34</sup>Hear ye, Jotuns! hear ye, Hrimthursar! sons of Suttung! also ye, Æsir's friends! how I forbid, how I prohibit man's joy unto the damsel, man's converse to the damsel. <sup>35</sup>Hrimgrimnir the Thurs is named, that shall possess thee, in the grating of the dead beneath; there shall wretched thralls, from the tree's roots, goats' water give thee. Other drink shalt thou, maiden! never get, either for thy pleasure, or for my pleasure.

translation.original_text: <sup>36</sup>"Hvat vanntu þá meðan, Þórr?" <sup>37</sup>"Brúðir berserkja / barðak í Hléseyju; / þær höfðu verst unnit, / vélta þjóð alla." <sup>38</sup>"Klæki vanntu þá, Þórr, / er þú á konum barðir." <sup>39</sup>"Vargynjur þat váru, / en varla konur, / skelldu skip mitt, / er ek skorðat hafðak, / ægðu mér járnlurki / en eltu Þjalfa. / Hvat vanntu meðan, Hárbarðr?" <sup>40</sup>"Ek vark í hernum, / er hingat gerðisk / gnæfa gunnfana, / geir at rjóða."
translation.text: <sup>36</sup>Thurs[38] I cut for thee, and three letters mere: ergi, and oedi, and othola. So will I cut them out, as I have cut them, in, if there need shall be. _Gerd_. <sup>37</sup>Hail rather to thee, youth! and accept an icy cup, filled with old mead; although I thought not that I ever should love one of Vanir race. _Skirnir_. <sup>38</sup>All my errand will I know, ere I hence ride home. When wilt thou converse hold with the powerful son of Niord? _Gerd_. <sup>39</sup>Barri the grove is named, which we both know, the grove of tranquil paths. Nine nights hence, there to Niord's son Gerd will grant delight. <sup>40</sup>Tell me, Skirnir! ere thou thy steed unsaddlest, and a foot hence thou goest, what thou hast accomplished in Jotunheim, for my pleasure or thine? _Skirnir_.

translation.original_text: <sup>41</sup>"Þess viltu nú geta, / er þú fórt oss óljúfan at bjóða." <sup>42</sup>"Bæta skal þér þat þá / munda baugi, / sem jafnendr unnu, / þeir er okkr vilja sætta." <sup>43</sup>"Hvar namtu þessi / in hnæfiligu orð, / er ek heyrða aldregi / in hnæfiligri?" <sup>44</sup>"Nam ek at mönnum / þeim inum aldrænum, / er búa í heimis skógum." <sup>45</sup>"Þó gefr þú gott nafn dysjum, / er þú kallar þær heimis skóga."
translation.text: <sup>41</sup>Barri the grove is named, which we both know, the grove of tranquil paths. Nine nights hence, there to Niord's son Gerd will grant delight. _Frey_. <sup>42</sup>Long is one night, yet longer two will be; how shall I three endure. Often a month to me less has seemed than half a night of longing. <sup>43</sup>Where didst thou learn words than which I never heard more irritating? _Harbard_. <sup>44</sup>From men I learned them, from ancient men, whose home is in the woods. _Thor_. <sup>45</sup>Thou givest certainly a good name to grave-mounds, when thou callest them, homes in the woods. _Harbard_.

translation.original_text: <sup>46</sup>"Svá dæmi ek of slíkt far." <sup>47</sup>"Orðkringi þín / mun þér illa koma, / ef ek ræð á vág at vaða; / ulfi hæra / hygg ek þik æpa munu, / ef þú hlýtr af hamri högg." <sup>48</sup>"Sif á hó heima, / hans muntu fund vilja, / þann muntu þrek drýgja, / þat er þér skyldara." <sup>49</sup>"Mælir þú at munns ráði, / svá at mér skyldi verst þykkja, / halr inn hugblauði, / hygg ek, at þú ljúgir." <sup>50</sup>"Satt hygg ek mik segja; / seinn ertu at för þinni, / langt myndir þú nú kominn, Þórr, / ef þú litum færir."
translation.text: <sup>46</sup>So speak I of such a subject. _Thor_. <sup>47</sup>Thy shrewd words will bring thee evil, if I resolve the sound to ford. Louder than a wolf thou wilt howl, I trow, if of my hammer thou gettest a touch. _Harbard_. <sup>48</sup>Sif has a gallant at home; thou wilt anxious be to find him: thou shalt that arduous work perform; it will beseem thee better. _Thor_. <sup>49</sup>Thou utterest what comes upmost, so that to me it be most annoying, thou dastardly varlet! I believe thou art lying. _Harbard_. <sup>50</sup>I believe I am telling truth. Thou art travelling slowly; thou wouldst have long since arrived, hadst thou assumed another form. _Thor_.

translation.original_text: <sup>51</sup>"Hárbarðr inn ragi, / heldr hefr þú nú mik dvalðan." <sup>52</sup>"Ása-þórs hugða ek / aldregi mundu / glepja féhirði farar." <sup>53</sup>"Ráð mun ek þér nú ráða; / ró þú hingat bátinum, / hættum hætingi, / hittu föður Magna." <sup>54</sup>"Farðu firr sundi, / þér skal fars synja." <sup>55</sup>"Vísa þú mér nú leiðina, / alls þú vill mik eigi um váginn ferja."
translation.text: <sup>51</sup>Harbard! thou wretch! rather is it thou who hast detained me. _Harbard_. <sup>52</sup>I never thought that a ferryman could the course of Asa-Thor retard. _Thor_. <sup>53</sup>One advice I now will give thee: row hither with thy boat; let us cease from threats; approach the sire of Magni. _Harbard_. <sup>54</sup>Go farther from the sound, the passage is refused thee. _Thor_. <sup>55</sup>Show me then the way, if thou wilt not ferry me across the water. _Harbard_.

translation.original_text: <sup>56</sup>Lítit er at synja, / langt er at fara; / stund er til stokksins, / önnur til steinsins, / haltu svá til vinstra vegsins, / unz þú hittir Verland; / þar mun Fjörgyn / hitta Þór, son sinn, / ok mun hon kenna hánum áttunga brautir / til Óðins landa." <sup>57</sup>"Mun ek taka þangat í dag?" <sup>58</sup>"Taka við víl ok erfiði, / at upprennandi sólu, / er ek get þána." <sup>59</sup>"Skammt mun nú mál okkat, / alls þú mér skætingu einni svarar; / launa mun ek þér farsynjun, / ef vit finnumk í sinn annat." <sup>60</sup>"Far þú nú, / þars þik hafi allan gramir."
translation.text: <sup>56</sup>That's too little to refuse. 'Tis far to go; 'tis to the stock an hour, and to the stone another; then keep the left hand way, until thou reachest Verland; there will Fiorgyn find her son Thor, and point out to him his kinsmen's ways to Odin's land. _Thor_. <sup>57</sup>Can I get there to-day? _Harbard_. <sup>58</sup>With pain and toil thou mayest get there, while the sun is up, which, I believe, is now nigh. _Thor_. <sup>59</sup>Our talk shall now be short, as thou answerest with scoffing only. For refusing to ferry me I will reward thee, if another time we meet. _Harbard_. <sup>60</sup>Just go to where all the powers of evil may have thee.
